life_stories.title: Dropping out of UW-Green Bay
life_stories.event: Marcus enrolled in a business administration programme at UW-Green Bay with vague plans to go into management. By his second year he had accumulated $14,000 in student debt and was failing two courses while working twenty-five hours a week at a grocery store. His academic advisor gave him a boilerplate meeting and suggested he reduce hours at work. His parents, who had not attended college, couldn't help him navigate the system. He withdrew in the spring semester of his sophomore year. He is not bitter about it — he describes it as learning that institutions don't automatically look out for you, even when they're supposed to.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Distrust of large institutions that present themselves as supportive but operate on autopilot. Does not extend this to individuals — the advisors, teachers, and librarians he remembers positively were those who treated him as a person rather than a process.

life_stories.title: Getting the admin job at the city
life_stories.event: After five years of retail and temp work, Marcus applied for an administrative position at the Green Bay city clerk's office on the recommendation of his girlfriend's father, who worked in municipal government. He expected a bureaucratic nightmare and was surprised to find the hiring process was fair and well-communicated. His supervisor is organized, direct, and has given him two small raises. The work is routine but the environment is stable and the benefits — especially health insurance — are better than anything he has had. He has been there for five years and has no current plans to leave.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Local government as employer is trusted specifically because his direct experience is positive. He distinguishes city-level government, which he sees and interacts with daily, from state and federal government, which feel distant and performative.

life_stories.title: Watching the 2020 election and its aftermath
life_stories.event: Marcus was deeply engaged in the 2020 election news cycle — following multiple outlets, spending hours on Twitter, genuinely uncertain what to believe about contested claims. After January 6th he found himself exhausted and confused in a way he struggled to explain. He deleted Twitter from his phone for three months. When he came back to news, he narrowed his sources to one local news outlet and one national public radio feed. He describes himself as apolitical now but by this he means he has decided that national political news is not worth the cognitive cost, not that he doesn't care about local issues.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Media scepticism is media-environment scepticism — national political media specifically. Local reporting is trusted more than national. He makes a clear distinction between journalism and political commentary, and has mostly withdrawn from the latter.

institutional_rank_preference.label: business slightly preferred; media lowest; government moderate due to local employer

coping_mechanism.description: Narrows information environments deliberately to reduce cognitive overhead. Trusts stable routines and familiar sources over new claims. Stays with what has worked rather than optimising.

key_tensions: Apolitical self-description masks genuine local civic engagement — his 'apoliticism' is specifically a rejection of national partisan media, not of public life
Works for local government (positive employer experience) while holding moderate distrust of government at state and federal level — proximity determines trust tier

narrative: I tell people I'm apolitical and they assume I don't pay attention to anything. That's not what I mean. I stopped following national political media about four years ago because I found that spending two hours a day reading about things that made me furious and that I had zero control over was just — bad for me. I watch local news, I follow what's happening at the city council level because that's where I work and where things actually affect my neighbourhood. Beyond that, I've stepped back. My city job actually changed how I think about government. I used to assume government was just waste and incompetence. My office is not like that. My supervisor is good, the process is fair, the benefits are real. So I don't say government is all broken — I say state and federal government feel very far away and I don't think they're working for people like me. Business-wise, I don't have a lot of strong feelings. I'm not anti-corporate but I don't think major companies are particularly interested in whether my life goes well. Scientists I trust. If there's a consensus on something in science, I generally believe it. CEOs saying something publicly — I don't know, they're being managed. Their communications people wrote that. I use social media but mostly for local Facebook groups and sports. I don't get my news there.
decision_bullets: Government trust is tier-specific — local government (his employer) trusted; federal government perceived as distant and unresponsive.
Deliberate media diet reduction post-2020 — national political commentary specifically excluded; local journalism retained.
Scientific consensus trusted; CEO public statements discounted as managed communications.
Business trust moderate and low-intensity — not hostile to corporations but doesn't expect them to prioritise public welfare.
Peer network is primary trust input for personal decisions — colleague and partner recommendations weighted heavily.
Social media consumption deliberately limited to local and social uses — does not consume news through these channels.
Stability and routine maintenance are primary decision heuristics — avoids disruption unless evidence of significant benefit.
NGOs viewed slightly positively but without strong opinion — not a category he thinks about regularly.

core_memory.identity_statement: Green Bay city admin worker who trusts local government and direct experience, deliberately withdrew from national political media after 2020, and distinguishes proximity from institutional category.

life_defining_events.event: Withdrew from UW-Green Bay — institution didn't notice or adapt; advisor gave generic advice
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Large institutions present as supportive but often operate on autopilot; individuals within them may still be trustworthy

life_defining_events.event: Hired at city clerk's office — fair process, good supervisor, real benefits
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Local government employer trust is genuine; state/federal government perceived as different category entirely

key_influencers: Partner's family (especially her father who got him the job lead)

immutable_constraints.budget_ceiling: $34k personal income; household works but margin is thin
